title: Context Engineering — Managing the Window
description: Context engineering is the discipline of efficiently using the LLM's context window — deciding what fits, what gets dropped, and how to structure the available space for maximum task performance.

sections.title: Context Window Fundamentals
sections.description:
Every LLM has a context window — the maximum number of tokens it can process in a single forward pass. This includes the prompt (system + user + retrieved context) and the generated output. Current generation models support 4K–200K tokens (GPT-4o: 128K, Claude 3.5: 200K, Gemini 1.5: 1M+).

Key constraints:
• Compute cost: Attention complexity is O(T²) per layer. Longer contexts mean slower inference and higher memory usage. FlashAttention mitigates this but does not eliminate the quadratic cost for generation.
• The 'lost in the middle' problem: Information placed in the middle of the context window is significantly less likely to influence the model's output than information at the beginning or end. This is a well-documented phenomenon (Liu et al., 2024).
• Recall degrades: Models reliably use information near the start and end of the context window but struggle with content in the middle third. The effect worsens with longer contexts.
sections.codeBlock:
# Context window token counting
import tiktoken

def count_tokens(text: str, model="gpt-4") -> int:
    """Count tokens for a given string."""
    enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model)
    return len(enc.encode(text))

def fits_in_window(prompt: str, output_tokens: int, max_window: int = 128_000) -> bool:
    """Check if prompt + expected output fits in context window."""
    return count_tokens(prompt) + output_tokens <= max_window

# Budget breakdown
SYSTEM_BUDGET = 500       # system prompt
USER_BUDGET = 200         # current query
OUTPUT_BUDGET = 4096      # max generation tokens
CONTEXT_BUDGET = 30000    # remaining for retrieved context / history

# ponytail: simple fixed budgets; real systems dynamically allocate
# based on the task's needs and the available window

sections.title: The 'Lost in the Middle' Problem
sections.description:
Liu et al. (2024) demonstrated a critical finding: LLM performance on tasks requiring information from the middle of the context is significantly worse than when information is at the start or end. For multi-document QA, accuracy drops by ~20% when the relevant document is in the middle vs at the edges.

Mitigations:
(1) Place critical info at the start or end: The system prompt and the most recent/relevant retrieved context should bookend the window.
(2) Structured context formatting: Use headers, numbered lists, or XML tags to make document boundaries clear. Models can attend to structure — a well-formatted context is more usable than a wall of text.
(3) Recursive summarization: Instead of stuffing 100 documents into the window, summarize each, summarize groups of summaries, and put only the top-level summary + top-K relevant documents in the window.

FAANG interview tip: When designing a long-context system, always mention the 'lost in the middle' finding and show how your design accounts for it. This separates senior from junior system designs.
sections.codeBlock:
# Context formatting to mitigate lost-in-the-middle

def format_rag_context(docs: list[dict]) -> str:
    """
    Format retrieved documents with clear structure.
    Most relevant doc goes FIRST (attention bias toward start).
    Second-most relevant goes LAST (recency bias).
    The rest go in the middle.
    """
    if len(docs) <= 2:
        ordered = docs
    else:
        # Place best doc first, worst in middle, second-best last
        ordered = [docs[0]] + docs[2:-1] + [docs[1]] if len(docs) > 2 else docs
    
    parts = []
    for i, doc in enumerate(ordered):
        parts.append(f"<document index=\"{i}\">\n{doc['text']}\n</document>")
    return "\n\n".join(parts)

# Usage
context = format_rag_context(retrieved_docs)
prompt = f"""<system>
Answer using ONLY the documents below.
</system>
{context}
<query>
{user_query}
</query>"""

sections.title: Sliding Window & Context Management Patterns
sections.description:
For multi-turn conversations or streaming document processing, you need strategies to manage the context window actively:

1. Fixed sliding window: Keep the last N turns of conversation. Drop older messages. Simple, works for chat, loses mid-conversation context. Most common heuristic: keep system prompt + last 10–20 turns.

2. Token-budget window: Count tokens per message. Drop the oldest messages first until the total fits under a budget. More predictable than turn-counting. You can also 'summarize and evict' — condense dropped messages into a single summary token.

3. Summarize-then-drop: Before an old message is evicted, run a summary step: 'Summarize the conversation so far in 100 words.' Keep the summary as a proxy. This preserves long-term context as a compressed representation. Used by systems like MemGPT/Letta.

4. Structured memory: Maintain explicit data structures (user profile, task list, key facts) outside the context window. Only inject relevant portions based on the current query. Most memory-efficient but requires an extraction step per turn.

sections.title: Context Caching & Prompt Caching
sections.description:
When the same context prefix is reused across many requests (e.g., a shared system prompt + document set), prompt caching dramatically reduces latency and cost. Supported natively by: Anthropic Claude (breakpoint-based, cache written on first use, hit on subsequent), OpenAI (automatic, transparent), and Google Gemini (context caching API).

Cache economics: Cache writes are typically ~25% more expensive than normal tokens, but cache reads are ~75% cheaper. Net benefit appears if you invoke the cached prefix at least 3–5 times. For a system prompt + 50K context used across 10K queries/day, caching reduces costs by ~60% and latency by ~40%.

Cache-aware prompt design: Structure prompts so the stable part (system, instructions, static reference docs) comes FIRST. Put the variable part (query, current turn) LAST. This maximizes cache hits. Treat the cache as a cache — don't repeat the stable prefix verbatim or the cache key changes.
sections.codeBlock:
# Anthropic Claude — explicit prompt caching
# import anthropic

# client = anthropic.Anthropic()
# response = client.messages.create(
#     model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
#     max_tokens=1024,
#     system=[
#         {
#             "type": "text",
#             "text": STABLE_SYSTEM_PROMPT,  # ← cached after first call
#             "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"}
#         }
#     ],
#     messages=[{"role": "user", "content": VARIABLE_QUERY}]  # ← NOT cached
# )

# # ponytail: cache is ephemeral (TTL ~5 min).
# # For longer retention, use the Anthropic caching API with a cache_key.

# OpenAI — automatic caching (no explicit control)
# Cache hits on prefix (system + first messages) when >1024 tokens
# Cache hit indicator: response.usage.prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens

def check_cache_efficiency(response) -> str:
    usage = response.usage
    cached = usage.prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens
    total = usage.prompt_tokens
    ratio = cached / total * 100 if total else 0
    return f"Cache hit ratio: {ratio:.1f}% ({cached}/{total} tokens)"

# Design prompts for cacheability
def cache_aware_prompt(system: str, context: str, query: str) -> list:
    # Stable prefix = system + pre-processed context
    # Variable suffix = user query only
    return [
        {"role": "system", "content": system},          # cached
        {"role": "user", "content": context},            # cached (if reused)
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "Understood."},  # cached
        {"role": "user", "content": query}               # NOT cached
    ]

sections.title: Long-Context vs RAG Trade-off
sections.description:
As context windows grow (GPT-4o: 128K, Gemini: 1M+, future: unlimited), the question arises: Why do we need RAG if the model can just read everything? The answer depends on the use case:

When to use long-context (just stuff everything in):
• Ad-hoc analysis of a single large document (legal contract, codebase)
• One-off research questions over a known corpus
• When latency isn't critical and cost is secondary

When to use RAG:
• Production cost: Long-context costs O(T²). A 100K token prompt is ~75¢ with GPT-4o vs ~0.3¢ for a RAG pipeline. At 10K queries/day, RAG saves ~$7K/day.
• Latency: Long-context prompts take 5–30s to process. RAG + short-context takes 1–3s.
• Precision: RAG retrieves only the top-K relevant chunks (~3K tokens). Long-context buries the relevant info in noise — accuracy can be lower due to the lost-in-the-middle effect.
• Dynamic data: RAG queries a live index that can be updated in real-time. Long-context requires regenerating the full prompt.

The hybrid approach: Use RAG as the default for production. Use long-context for specific tasks where recall of rare facts in a known corpus is critical. In system design interviews, always start with RAG and add long-context as a specialized tool.
sections.codeBlock:
# Cost comparison: long-context vs RAG
MODEL_COST_PER_INPUT_TOKEN = 10 / 1_000_000   # GPT-4o: $10/1M input tokens
EMBEDDING_COST_PER_QUERY = 0.00001             # text-embedding-3-small: ~$0.00001/query
STORAGE_COST = 0                               # negligible for FAISS in-memory

def long_context_cost(docs_tokens: int, queries_per_day: int) -> float:
    """Cost of stuffing all docs into each prompt."""
    per_query = docs_tokens * MODEL_COST_PER_INPUT_TOKEN
    return per_query * queries_per_day * 365  # annual cost

def rag_cost(top_k_tokens: int, queries_per_day: int) -> float:
    """Cost of querying only top-K relevant chunks."""
    per_query = (top_k_tokens * MODEL_COST_PER_INPUT_TOKEN
                 + EMBEDDING_COST_PER_QUERY)
    return per_query * queries_per_day * 365

# Example: 50K tokens of documentation, 10K queries/day
long = long_context_cost(50_000, 10_000)
rag = rag_cost(3_000, 10_000)
print(f"Long-context annual: \${long:,.0f}")
print(f"RAG annual: \${rag:,.0f}")
print(f"Savings with RAG: \${long - rag:,.0f}/year")
# Long-context annual: $1,825,000
# RAG annual:         $  109,500
# Savings:            $1,715,500/year
# ponytail: ignores cache hits (which would reduce long-context costs ~60%)

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts (Liu et al., 2024) — Critical paper: LLMs lose information in the middle of long contexts, FAANG interview favourite
  • Context Engineering for LLMs — Structured approach to designing and optimizing LLM context windows
  • Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM) for Code Generation — Context transformation for code infilling tasks
  • Lilian Weng — Prompt Engineering — OpenAI researcher's comprehensive guide to context and prompt design
  • Anthropic Cookbook — Context engineering patterns for Claude, including long-context best practices

details:
The 3-zone context model: Think of the context window as three zones: (1) System zone (start) — instructions, persona, rules; (2) Working memory (recent) — current task, few-shot examples, most relevant retrieved docs; (3) Archive (middle) — older history, less relevant context. The archive zone has the weakest influence on the output. Design your systems to minimize reliance on the archive zone — use structured memory or external retrieval instead.

Interview framework: When asked to design a long-context system in a FAANG interview: (1) Identify the context budget (window size — system — output — safety margin). (2) Categorize content into 'must-have' (system, query, top docs), 'nice-to-have' (history), and 'evictable' (old context). (3) Propose a strategy for each zone: summarize evicted content, cache stable prefixes, structure must-have content to minimize lost-in-the-middle. (4) Evaluate the cost, latency, and accuracy trade-offs. This framework alone demonstrates senior-level systems thinking.
